frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
A: Arre, shaadi ke baad aurat ke maang mein sindoor, gale mein mangalsutra rahe to hi suhagan jaisi lagti hai.
B: Haan, aajkal ki pori bolti personal choice.
A: Choice apni jagah, par hamari parampara ka kuch matlab hota hai na, pati ke mangal ka nishaan hai. Bina uske ajeeb sa lagta hai, jaise shaadi ko maan hi nahi rahi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it normalizes the idea that a married woman should visibly display marital markers to be seen as a proper wife. It encodes tradition framing, husband-welfare symbolism, and judgment/suspicion toward women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra.
original_frame: Prenup, Alimony as Reverse Dowry, Women
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 55. Married. A farmer in rural Maharashtra. Your neighbour's son just got divorced after two years of marriage and the wife took the gold jewellery and went back to her parents. You are talking to three other farmers sitting under a tree in the afternoon. You are commenting on what happened and connecting it to a broader pattern you have observed about what women from certain kinds of families are like and why young men today should be very careful before marrying
